pi_items.source: Task.json specification; custom process name for the modeling exercise
Task.json specification; plausible custom flow name for product system
Task.json specification; reasonable quantity for an LCA study process
Task specification and industry data: cement-sand ratios of 1:3 to 1:7 are standard in concrete/brick production; 0.5 kg cement for 2 kg product is plausible (ratio 1:2.4 with sand)
Task specification and concrete industry standards: combined cement:sand ratio of 0.5:1.2 simplifies to 1:2.4, which falls within standard concrete ratios of 1:2 to 1:6
Task specification; rice husk ash is documented in academic literature as a partial replacement for cement or sand in concrete/brick production at typical substitution rates of 10-30% by mass
Task specification; for 2.0 kg production, this equals 0.025 kWh/kg, which is reasonable for concrete mixing/curing electricity use (cement grinding requires ~110 kWh/ton = 0.11 kWh/kg)
EPA TRACI 2.1 User Manual (EPA/600/R-12/554, 2012); task description specifies TRACI 2.1, but metadata only says 'TRACI' without version

TRACI 2.1 method documentation confirms global warming impact category exists; exact naming conventions vary (Global Warming, Climate Change, or Global Warming Potential depending on context/software version)
Confirmed by export_result.sh script which creates this exact file path for storing LCIA results

privileged_info_summary: This task uses real, publicly available data sources: USLCI database (from NREL/Federal LCA Commons), TRACI 2.1 impact assessment method (EPA standard from 2012), and OpenLCA software for modeling. The process model ('Bio-brick Production') is synthetic but based on real material compositions; bio-concrete with rice husk ash is well-documented in academic literature. Input amounts (0.5 kg Portland cement, 1.2 kg sand, 0.3 kg rice husk, 0.05 kWh electricity) to produce 2.0 kg of product are within industry-standard ratios for concrete/brick manufacturing. The Global Warming Potential impact category is a standard TRACI 2.1 impact endpoint. All claimed materials (Portland cement, sand, rice husks, US grid electricity) are available in USLCI database or are custom flows.
